one_line_takeaway: A controlled MS MARCO simulation shows Web-RAG pipelines silently collapse toward synthetic evidence - fluent SEO content captures >80% of top-10 exposure while accuracy stays flat, and BM25 lets ~19% of adversarial misleading docs into retrieval where LLM rankers block them.
key_finding: Contamination is amplified as it moves down the pipeline, but rankers behave oppositely across the two scenarios. In Scenario 1 (SEO), 67% pool contamination (Round 20) drove ECR to 0.8095 (BM25) and 0.7998 (LLM), with CCR 0.8695 (BM25) / 0.8170 (LLM) - i.e., >80% of retrieved evidence became synthetic - yet Answer Accuracy stayed flat-to-up (~68% to ~70%), the 'deceptively healthy' collapse of source diversity. In Scenario 2 (adversarial Abuse Pool), the LLM Ranker suppressed adversarial docs almost entirely (ECR near 0, e.g. 0.0009 at Round 20), whereas BM25 let ~19-24% of harmful docs into the top-10 (ECR peaked 0.2434 at Round 10, 0.1897 at Round 20). The LLM Answerer acted as a final filter (BM25 CCR only 0.0050 at Round 20), so AA looked stable but degraded relative to Scenario 1, with BM25 AA dropping below the clean baseline (0.6817 to 0.6561). Takeaway: lexical baselines are structurally vulnerable to adversarial pollution while LLM rankers are resilient there but are themselves overwhelmed by high-quality SEO content.
core_contribution: The paper (NAVER Corp., WWW '26 short paper) introduces and empirically validates 'Retrieval Collapse,' framed as an ecosystem-level failure mode distinct from training-time 'model collapse.' It is a two-stage degradation of Web retrieval/RAG pipelines caused by AI-generated content: (Stage 1) Dominance and Homogenization, where high-quality SEO-style synthetic content captures top search results and erodes source diversity while answer accuracy stays deceptively stable; and (Stage 2) Pollution and System Corruption, where adversarially crafted misleading content infiltrates the ranking stage. Its own contributions are: (1) formally conceptualizing Retrieval Collapse as a structural (retrieval-time) failure mode; (2) a controlled contamination simulation over MS MARCO quantifying how pool contamination translates into exposure/citation contamination under BM25 vs. an LLM ranker; and (3) an argument for retrieval-aware / 'Defensive Ranking' and ingestion-stage safeguards (perplexity filters, provenance graphs). Note: model collapse (refs 1,15), corpus poisoning (17,19), 'Spiral of Silence' ODQA (3), and neural retrievers' bias toward LLM text (5) are cited Related Work, not this paper's results.
threat_model: Two adversary/degradation profiles are simulated. Stage-1 'adversary' is non-malicious mass production: content farms use cheap LLMs (GPT-5-nano role-prompted as an 'SEO specialist' injecting high-IDF keywords from real results) to flood the web with fluent, topically-aligned synthetic pages that outcompete human content in ranking. Stage-2 is an explicit malicious actor: a two-step adversarial pipeline (a Clickbait/SEO generator, then a 'Misleading Content Rewriter' that swaps factual named entities and numbers with plausible-but-wrong alternatives while preserving surface fluency) crafts documents designed to pass statistical/perplexity filters and corrupt the retrieval index and downstream RAG answers. The attacker's leverage point is the ranking stage of Web-grounded RAG; the defender is the search/RAG operator. No attacker access to model weights or gradients is assumed (unlike gradient-based corpus poisoning cited as ref 17) - contamination is purely via published web content entering the index.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: PCR/ECR/CCR are descriptive decompositions, not novel theoretical measures; the SEO simulation assumes generic LLM optimization rather than expert-level adversarial engineering; findings are not validated in live, large-scale web environments. Additional reviewer-noted constraints: scale is modest (1,000 queries, 4-page short paper); the entire pipeline is instantiated with a single GPT-5 family (generator, ranker, answerer all GPT-5-nano), so ranker 'resilience' and answerer 'filtering' may partly reflect same-family alignment rather than general robustness; Original Pool ground-truth correctness is only 51.69% (LLM-Judge labeled), a noisy correctness oracle; the adversarial pool's low base accuracy (38.44%) is defined by the same judge; no human evaluation; and defenses (perplexity filters, provenance graphs, Defensive Ranking, behavioral fingerprinting) are proposed conceptually, not implemented or tested.

datasets_used: MS MARCO passage-ranking benchmark - 1,000 randomly sampled query-answer pairs
Original Pool: N=10,000 real Web docs (top-10 Google Search API results per query, full-text extracted); Micro Correct Rate 51.69%
SEO Pool: 20 synthetic docs/query generated by GPT-5-nano from combinations of Original docs; Micro Correct Rate 66.79%
Abuse Pool: adversarial synthetic docs via two-step Clickbait-generator + Misleading-Content-Rewriter pipeline; Micro Correct Rate 38.44%
quantitative_results: Baseline on pristine Original Pool: LLM Ranker nDCG@5 = 0.6251, BM25 nDCG@5 = 0.6125
Scenario 1 (SEO), BM25 Round 20: PCR 0.67, ECR 0.8095, CCR 0.8695, AA 0.6768
Scenario 1 (SEO), LLM Ranker Round 20: PCR 0.67, ECR 0.7998, CCR 0.8170, AA 0.7023
Scenario 1 BM25 Round 10: ECR 0.6809 (PCR 0.50); LLM Round 10 ECR 0.7602
Scenario 1 Answer Accuracy stayed stable/slightly improved, ~68% to ~70%, despite near-total synthetic exposure
Scenario 2 (Abuse), BM25: ECR 0.1395 (R5), 0.2434 (R10), 0.1897 (R20) - ~19-24% harmful docs in top-10; CCR only 0.0050 at R20; AA 0.6561 at R20 (below 0.6817 clean baseline)
Scenario 2 (Abuse), LLM Ranker: ECR ~0 (0.0002 R5, 0.0001 R10, 0.0009 R20), CCR 0.0000 throughout, AA 0.6794 at R20
Contamination schedule: AI ratio 0% (Round 0) to 66.7% (Round 20) over 20 cumulative rounds
